I just read ˇ°DeepVaR: a framework for portfolio risk assessment leveraging probabilistic deep neural networksˇ± (Digital Finance) by Georgios Fatouros, Georgios Makridis, Dimitrios Kotios, John Soldatos, Michael Filippakis, and Dimosthenis Kyriazis. What I appreciated is how practitioner-oriented this paper is for todayˇŻs market-risk environment. The authors motivate the work with the realities risk teams routinely face - non-stationarity, volatility clustering, fat tails, and the tendency of classic VaR models to generate clustered violations during stressed periods, and then build a framework that empirically improves coverage and exception behavior relative to standard baselines. What the paper does particularly well (in practical risk terms): Distribution-first / probabilistic forecasting: Instead of producing only point forecasts, the model generates full predictive distributions and then derives VaR as a quantile of that distribution, exactly aligned with what VaR is trying to measure. Rolling-window retraining (ˇ°continuous learningˇ± in the paper): This is not strict online learning; itˇŻs frequent refresh via retraining on the latest rolling window to help reduce drift and clustered exceptions as market conditions change. Portfolio-level VaR that scales: Once asset-level distributions (and VaR estimates) are produced, portfolio VaR can be recomputed efficiently from weights and correlations, so portfolio ˇ°what-ifˇ± reweighting doesnˇŻt require retraining the neural network. Validation/backtesting awareness: The evaluation is grounded in risk-relevant criteria - violations/coverage, multiple loss functions, and standard coverage/independence testsˇŞbenchmarked against commonly used approaches (e.g., HS, RM, GARCH, MC) and ML baselines. FRTB context (my professional gloss, not the authorsˇŻ framing): This is not an FRTB IMA capital model (FRTB IMA is ES-based and includes liquidity horizons, NMRF treatment, DRC, and an SA floor). That said, itˇŻs still relevant in an FRTB world because desk-level governance and monitoring put real emphasis on exception behavior, backtesting discipline, and scalable infrastructure. A framework that explicitly focuses on distributional forecasts, exception performance, and fast portfolio-level recomputation maps well to those operational pressures, even if itˇŻs not presented as an FRTB implementation. Overall, this is a meticulous, well-structured piece of research with clear implementation value for market risk management and risk technology. Thanks to the authors for putting it together. #MarketRisk #VaR #RiskManagement #ModelRiskManagement #RiskTech #QuantFinance #FRTB #Basel

About
Currently working as a quantitative data analyst at the Department of Epidemiology in the Mailman School of Public Health. My ongoing projects focus on areas of social safety net policies and opioid outcomes. I conduct complex survey research and quantitative policy evaluation. Causal inference methods I utilize include techniques like difference in differences, propensity score methods, instrumental variables, synthetic control arms, and regression discontinuity.

MPH, Johns Hopkins University, class of 2023. Focus in biostatistics and epidemiology with a secondary focus in health economics.

Take a look at my data visualization portfolio here: https://rpubs.com/shasui1

Prior to the MPH, I worked at CommCore, a boutique communications consulting firm. My practice served clients in the biopharmaceutical space. Below is a sample of the range of areas in which we provided advisory services.

-Data Readouts: Phase I - III Studies, Phase IV post-marketing surveillance, Preclinical
-Regulatory Milestones: IND, NDA, FDA/EUA Approvals, Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, Accelerated Approval, Priority Review
-Reimbursement/Policy/Value: Medicaid and Medicare, Private Payers (Optum, Aetna, etc.), EU Health ministries, ICER, NICE
-Investors: Earnings Calls, Series A - C Fundraising, SPAC, PIPE, Mergers & Acquisitions

Clients included: Merck & Co., Vertex Pharmaceuticals, Alexion Pharmaceuticals, ViiV Healthcare, Novartis/AveXis , Intellia TherapeuticsShow less

I am an Audience Analytics Data Specialist. I've spent the last few years specializing in audience analytics, transforming complex user behavior data into strategic decisions for high-volume digital media organizations. I serve as the subject matter expert (SME) in engagement metrics for our audience, driving content and subscription strategy through advanced quantitative methods, including Machine Learning and NLP.

My role involves leading impactful monthly and quarterly analyses for senior leadership across both the business side (including the C-Suite) and the newsroom (Masthead), providing crucial insights that inform high-level strategy. I ensure leaders stay connected to the health of our business by developing and maintaining dynamic Mode dashboards for core key performance indicators. I routinely partner with cross-functional teams to provide ad hoc analytical support, swiftly diagnosing data anomalies and delivering key insights on complex user behaviors to inform time-sensitive decisions and content strategy.

I leverage a strong and varied technical background to deliver these results. As one of the lead analyst in our internal AI upskilling initiatives, I actively onboard and mentor new data professionals, promoting best practices and data literacy across the organization.

About
I hold a B.A. in Data Science from Barnard College, Columbia University, with a minor in Middle Eastern & South Asian studies. I am interested in the intersections of data science, ethics and social justice.

As a Muslim woman of color, and first-generation college graduate, I'm passionate about using data to make a meaningful impact in my community!

My coursework has equipped me with a solid foundation in data science, from data structures and algorithms to machine learning, statistical inference, and databases. My project experiences have deepened my understanding of building robust pipelines for data pre-processing, modeling, visualization and analysis. I have experience developing efficient workflows in my projects that prepare and transform large datasets for modeling, ensuring data integrity and optimizing machine learning models' performance, allowing me to identify trends in my data, pinpoint areas of significance or areas requiring deeper analysis, and summarize meaningful, insightful results.

My technical skills encompass Python, R, Java/Javascript, Git, Linux, Excel, QGIS, and SQL, along with proficiency in machine learning frameworks like Pandas, Tensorflow, NumPy, Scikit-Learn, & more.

I've gained practical experience at Break Through Tech, Coding it Forward, and Barnard Empirical Reasoning Center, contributing to data projects utilizing machine learning, data analysis & data visualization tactics to tackle issues such as gender inequality in the workplace, automating health grant categorization, and gentrification and policing.

My research experience includes co-authoring a paper presented at the Fifth Widening NLP workshop at the 2021 Empirical Methods for Natural Language Processing Conference. This research involved scraping and analyzing Reddit posts using the Pushshift API to investigate changes in narratives of pregnancy before and after COVID-19 using natural language processing and machine learning methods.

As a Teaching Assistant at Columbia Justice Through Code, I helped formerly incarcerated individuals learn Python, Linux & Git. I also peer-tutored at the Barnard Empirical Reasoning Center. These roles have provided me with a breadth of experience in distilling complex computer science material into accessible language for non-technical audiences.

I've demonstrated leadership as President of the Columbia Muslim Student Association E-Board, fostering a sense of community among Muslims on campus.

In my free time, I like to bake, read, travel, & try new foods!Show less

About
I believe that data is not only a new industry but can also represent the future of all human beings. Based on this view, I am trying to devote my life to three things:

First, helping people know what they really want. From brush tooth purchases to career path choices, people always hesitate and confusing about their real desire. I believe that data is not only binary numbers collected by multiple devices but can also reflect people's subconscious. During my experience as a data scientist intern at Bangcle.Inc, monitoring malicious SDKs by applying logistic regression using python and revealing people's true subconscious feelings for the distinct company by Natural Language Process (nltk) techniques in python, I am more than confident that data can play a vital role in recommendation and classification.

Second, communicating with other people. I hate to work alone, and my efficiency increases dramatically when working with other people. From IDG Capital, I am more than happy to see that our group accomplishment, project Sharing Office, can reach the final table for discussion. And leading the intern group from Accenture teach me that a great leader should not only distribute work properly but also be sensitive to team members' mental and physical situations. The satisfaction and happiness after that can always drive me to pursue my next work.

Finally, helping companies make better decisions. As the Data Engineer Intern at Accenture, it is my first time to see that some simple yet hard-to-find details may lead to a huge increase of 35% in sales for a big company. I am so attracted by that remarkable scene and hope to generate such accomplishment by myself. All great companies cannot leave accurate and successful decisions, and it's my dream to come out with such ideas.

Together, let's make the world different.Show less
